figures.caption: Figure 2: An illustration of different flow states over a high-speed spiked cylinder (Λ = D{2L).
Figure 3: paq Spike-cylinder model installed in the RNHWT test section. pbq A line drawing of the spike-cylinder model (not to scale).

Figure 4: A sequence of schlieren snapshots over one cycle of pulsation for Λ = 0.5 and M8 = 6. The cycle consists of three phases: axial inflation (a to d); radial inflation (d to i); collapse (i to u). Arrows marked over the bow shock indicate its instantaneous direction of motion.

Figure 5: paq Pressure signal recorded on the cylinder face at a radial distance of D{8 for Λ = 0.5 over a representative period of five pulsation cycles. pbq Pressure variation in one pulsation cycle, corresponding to the schlieren data shown in figure 4; the blue-colored markers and associated labels correspond to the time instants at which schlieren snapshots are shown in figure 4.

Figure 6: (a) A magnified view of figure 4(a); (b) a schematic illustration of key flow features seen in (a). (c) A magnified view of figure 4(b); (d) a schematic illustration of key flow features seen in (c).

Figure 7: (a) A magnified view of figure 4(e); (b) a schematic illustration of key flow features seen in (a). (c) A magnified view of figure 4(g); (d) a schematic illustration of key flow features seen in (c).

Figure 8: (a) A magnified view of figure 4(k); (b) a schematic illustration of key flow features seen in (a). (c) A magnified view of figure 4(r); (d) a schematic illustration of key flow features seen in (c).
Figure 9: (a) SPOD modal energy spectra for Λ = 0.5; (b) Power spectral density (P̂ ) of nondimensionalized pressure fluctuations p1 {P0 for Λ = 0.5.
Figure 10: Probability density function (PDF) for maximum pressure pmax{P0 over 2000 pulsation cycles for Λ “ 0.5. The vertical dashed line at 0.0605 marks the most probable value of pmax{P0.

Figure 11: Maximum pressure pmax{P0 during pulsation for different Λ configurations. Red markers indicate experimental measurements, and the dashed line represents the average value across the six experimental data points.

Figure 12: A schematic representation of the axial inflation model. (a) shows the initial condition for the Riemann problem and (b) shows a representative time instant during the evolution of the solution to the Riemann problem.
Figure 13: (a) Initial conditions for the axial inflation phase; (b) idealized one-dimensional flow in the axial inflation phase.
Figure 14: Flow properties at the beginning of the axial inflation phase.
Figure 15: A comparison of predicted non-dimensional axial inflation time t˚ a ` “ TaU8 D ˘ with experiments.

Figure 16: (a) x-t diagram for the expansion wave reflection from the cylinder wall during the axial inflation phase. HE and TE are the head and tail of the expansion fan, respectively. C` and C´ are positive and negative characteristic lines. (b) Pressure variation along the spike length at the end of the axial inflation phase, t = Ta, for Λ “ 0.5.

Figure 18: (a) The variation of shock decay coefficient σ with shock strength η for cylindrical shock waves; (b) the variation of shock radius Rs{R0 with shock strength η for cylindrical shock waves, γ “ 1.4.

Figure 19: Initial conditions for the radial inflation phase: (a) key flow features at the beginning of the radial inflation phase; (b) modeled cylindrical region a1b1c1d1 at the onset of the radial inflation phase.
Figure 20: A comparison of predicted non-dimensional radial inflation time t˚ r ` “ TrU8 D ˘ with experiments.

Figure 21: (a) Pressure distribution downstream of the cylindrical shock for Λ = 0.5 and shock strength η = η2; (b) Density distribution downstream of the cylindrical shock for Λ = 0.5 and shock strength η = η2. η = η2 corresponds to the shock radius Rs = r0+R at the end of the radial inflation phase. p1 and ρ1 are the pressure and density immediately downstream of the cylindrical shock, respectively.
Figure 22: A schematic representation of the proposed model for the collapse phase. The head and tail of the expansion fan are labeled HE and TE, respectively.
Figure 23: Qualitative pressure profile along the spike during the collapse phase at the time instant where the expansion fan head meets the bow shock.
Figure 24: A comparison of predicted non-dimensional collapse time t˚ c ` “ TcU8 D ˘ with experiments.
